title: Apple and Microsoft miss earnings expectations, triggering Nasdaq's worst day since 2022
topicContext: EUR/USD is affected by equity market moves because large sell-offs in U.S. equities can trigger risk-off flows that paradoxically strengthen the dollar (as global investors repatriate capital to the U.S.) or weaken it (if the sell-off is seen as a U.S.-specific problem that reduces Fed hiking expectations). A broad tech earnings disappointment is particularly significant because it can shift the narrative about U.S. economic exceptionalism — the thesis that the U.S. economy is outperforming the rest of the world, which has been a key pillar of dollar strength. If U.S. corporate earnings start disappointing while European earnings hold up, the relative growth story shifts in the euro's favor.
body:
Shares of Apple and Microsoft tumbled in after-hours trading on Thursday after both companies reported quarterly earnings that missed Wall Street expectations, dragging Nasdaq futures down more than 3% and setting up what analysts warned could be the worst trading day for the tech-heavy index since September 2022.

Apple reported revenue of $89.2 billion for its fiscal second quarter, below the $91.8 billion consensus estimate, as iPhone sales in China fell 12% year-over-year amid intensifying competition from Huawei and other domestic manufacturers. The company's services division — which investors have increasingly relied on as the growth engine — grew 9%, below the 13% analysts had expected.

Microsoft's Azure cloud revenue grew 26%, below the 29% growth analysts had forecast and the slowest pace since 2022. The company also said it would increase capital expenditure on AI infrastructure by $4 billion in the current quarter, raising concerns about the return on the industry's massive AI investment cycle. CEO Satya Nadella said on the earnings call that 'the monetization of AI workloads is progressing but will take time to reach scale.'

The combined market capitalization loss across both companies exceeded $400 billion in after-hours trading. Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta all fell in sympathy, with the 'Magnificent Seven' group of mega-cap tech stocks losing approximately $700 billion in value.

The sell-off extended to Asian markets on Friday morning, with Japan's Nikkei 225 falling 2.8% and South Korea's KOSPI down 2.1%. European futures pointed to a lower open but by a smaller margin, with the Euro Stoxx 50 indicated down 1.1%.

Bond markets rallied as investors fled to safety, with the 10-year Treasury yield falling to 4.38% from 4.52%. The dollar weakened against the yen and Swiss franc — traditional safe-haven currencies — but was broadly steady against the euro.

Analysts said the earnings misses raise fundamental questions about the sustainability of the AI investment cycle. 'The market has been pricing in a scenario where AI spending immediately translates into revenue growth,' said one portfolio manager. 'What Apple and Microsoft are telling you is that the revenue uplift is slower and less certain than the bulls assumed. That repricing has barely begun.'
